results.possibleIdentity: Shelley Levine was a key administrative and technical staff member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she served as the Director of Technical Services for the organization. In this role, she was responsible for matching technical requests from artists with appropriate engineers and scientists. She was a prominent figure in the implementation of major E.A.T. projects, including the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, and the Anand Project in India (1969), which explored the use of instructional television in rural education. Levine is also credited with documenting many of the organization's activities through photography and film, and her contributions are noted in the Experiments in Art and Technology records archived at the Getty Research Institute.

results.possibleIdentity: Howard Gutstadt is a pioneering American video artist, filmmaker, and activist who was a central figure in the early video art and community media movements of the late 1960s and 1970s. In 1970, he co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City alongside Ken Marsh and Elliot Glass. The collective was one of the first to utilize portable Sony PortaPak cameras for social activism and community storytelling, documenting counterculture events such as early women's liberation marches and gay pride parades. Gutstadt is particularly noted for capturing the 1971 'Camp Jened tapes,' which provided the primary archival footage for the award-winning 2020 documentary 'Crip Camp.' Following PVT, he co-founded Survival Arts Media (SAM), which focused on electronic video art, immersive multimedia performances, and the creative use of video synthesizers like the Rutt-Etra and EAB (Electronic Associates of Berkeley). He was also instrumental in the rollout of public access television on cable in New York. In 1978, Gutstadt relocated to Berkeley, California, where he founded Digital Image Productions and worked with Sony and Autodesk to develop software tools for digital artists. His work has been exhibited at SIGGRAPH and is preserved in the NYU Special Collections Library.

results.possibleIdentity: Yehuda Ben-Yehuda (1932/1933–2010) was an Iraqi-born Israeli artist and sculptor active in New York City and Israel during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Baghdad, he moved to Israel at age 11 and later established himself in the New York art scene. He is notably recognized for his provocative sculptural work, which included life-sized human figures cast in latex from live models. In some of these works, such as those shown in the 'Tazpit' exhibition in Israel, he utilized electric appliances to make the latex bodies tremble, reflecting an intersection of art and technology consistent with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Ben-Yehuda was also a key figure in the early development of the SoHo art district in Manhattan; he owned a loft building at 141 Prince Street which he eventually sold to become one of the neighborhood's first artist cooperatives. In the early 1970s, he moved to Woodstock, New York, where he designed and hand-built a unique four-story home using 18 different types of wood harvested from his property. He remained active as a 'character' in the Woodstock community until his return to Israel later in life, where he died in 2010.

results.possibleIdentity: Dov Howard is a fictional character in the 'The Illuminatus! Trilogy' (1975), a series of satirical novels by Robert Shea and Robert Anton Wilson written between 1969 and 1971. In the narrative, Dov Howard is identified as the leader of the American Anarchist Association. The trilogy is deeply embedded in the counterculture and art scenes of the late 1960s and early 1970s and frequently references real organizations, conspiracy theories, and entities like the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). The authors themselves were active in this milieu, working as associate editors for Playboy magazine and participating in the Discordian movement. While no historical artist by the exact name 'Dov Howard' appears in the archives of E.A.T. or the National Center for Experiments in Television (NCET), the name appears prominently in these books which were influential within the underground art and technology circles of that era.

The name 'Dov Howard' may be a conflation of two individuals associated with technology and authorship during the 1960s and 1970s: Dov Frohman and Robert Howard. Dov Frohman (Dov Frohman-Bentchkowsky) is a renowned Israeli electrical engineer who worked at Bell Laboratories and Fairchild Semiconductor in the late 1960s, inventing the EPROM (Electrically Programmable Read-Only Memory) in 1971 while at Intel. Bell Labs engineers were the founding technical collaborators of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Robert Howard is a writer on technology and management who co-authored the book 'Leadership the Hard Way' (2008) with Frohman. Some web search results for 'Dov Howard' point specifically to this authorship duo or the book itself, which discusses the 'art' of leadership and organizational innovation.
